story:
Ben’s new ground-floor unit in the pristine Aperture building runs him like a program—lights that mimic sunrise, coffee that brews on schedule, air tuned for productivity. Clean, simple, efficient. After a month, the loneliness hums like a second climate. That’s when he starts seeing her.

At first she’s a flicker in the glass: a young woman in a faded floral dress, wiping a spotless table, then dissolving into pixels. A glitch, he tells himself. But she returns—quietly knitting in his armchair, watering a plant that isn’t there, standing at the window with a sadness he can’t name. He runs every diagnostic the apartment offers. Cameras show empty rooms. Sensors register no mass or heat. To the system, she doesn’t exist. He maps her appearances. She becomes routine, a gentle ghost in his sterile box at street level.

One rainy afternoon, the silence presses hard. He speaks. “Hello?” She jolts as if he’d fired a gun. The air shivers. She turns, eyes wide with real fear, and whispers, “You can see me?” The question changes everything. Not a recording. Not a haunting. A person.

They talk across an impossible gap. Her name is Anna. She’s in her living room in 1988, in the small house her husband, Jack, built. Ben is in 2035, in the apartment tower built on the lot where her house once stood. Same coordinates. Different years. They are each other’s ghosts.

Discovery becomes a map. “My sofa is here,” she says, patting air between his coffee table and media console. He holds up his translucent tablet; to her, it looks like rippling water. For the first time since moving in, he isn’t alone.

“How is this possible?” he asks. Anna frowns. Jack, an atmospheric physicist, used to talk about temporal resonance—thin places where time echoes. He believed their land sat on one. He was terrified someone would try to exploit it. He quit his job at Northlight Dynamics after its buyout by a larger conglomerate he called dangerous.

The name Northlight hits Ben like cold water. He pulls up the Aperture prospectus. The developer, Continuum, is a massive tech firm that acquired Northlight in the late ’80s. He looks around and sees the apartment differently. The ultrasonic sensors, the ceiling cameras, the always-on mic, the “environmental array” that never sleeps—none of this is for comfort. It’s instrumentation.

This isn’t a home. It’s a listening post aimed at an anomaly. Anna is the echo. He, with his remote job and spotless credit, is the controlled subject. Continuum didn’t just rent him a unit; they placed a probe, curious to learn whether the echo could see them back—and whether a bridge could be built.

He looks at Anna, her image wavering at the edge of his reality, not a specter to banish but a person to be captured by someone else’s project. His high-tech refuge isn’t haunted by the past. It’s haunted by a corporation reaching through time. And the worst part is simple, awful, and true: he paid for the privilege.
